variants: attractor: a stable region or state toward which a dynamical system tends to evolve
attractor: a state or region that nearby trajectories repeatedly move toward
attractor: a persistent destination pattern in the evolution of a dynamical system
basin of attraction: the set of initial states that flow toward the same attractor
basin of attraction: the region of starting conditions that end up at one stable pattern
basin of attraction: all states whose dynamics carry them into a shared attractor
phase space: a space of possible states and trajectories of a dynamical system
phase space: the coordinate space that represents every possible state of a system
phase space: a geometric state space where system trajectories can be traced
fixed point: a state that remains unchanged under the system dynamics
fixed point: a state that maps back to itself when the dynamics are applied
fixed point: an unchanged equilibrium state of a transformation or flow
attractor network: a cognitive or neural network whose activity settles into stable memory or perception states
attractor network: a neural system where activity patterns converge toward stable representational states
attractor network: a model of cognition in which recurrent activity falls into learned stable basins
prototype: a central or typical example around which category judgments are organized
prototype: a representative center that makes some category members feel more typical than others
prototype: an idealized reference case used to judge similarity within a category
schema: an organized cognitive structure for interpreting situations and expectations
schema: a mental frame that organizes expectations, roles, and likely events
schema: a structured interpretive pattern used to make sense of experience
conceptual space: a geometric representation of meanings using dimensions, distances, regions, and prototypes
conceptual space: a metric meaning space where concepts occupy regions along quality dimensions
conceptual space: a geometry of concepts in which distance and region structure encode similarity
semantic distance: a measure of how far apart meanings or concepts are in a representational space
semantic distance: the separation between concepts in a meaning representation
semantic distance: a distance-like quantity that tracks how dissimilar two meanings are
family resemblance: category membership based on overlapping similarities rather than a single essence
family resemblance: classification by shared patterns of similarity without one necessary feature
family resemblance: category structure built from crisscrossing similarities among examples
embedding: a vector representation that preserves useful relationships among inputs
embedding: a learned vector form that places related inputs near one another
embedding: a numeric representation where geometric relations carry semantic or functional information
activation vector: a direction or state in a neural network activation space
activation vector: a pattern of neural network activity represented as coordinates in feature space
activation vector: a vector-valued internal state produced by a model layer
steering vector: an activation-space direction used to control model behavior
steering vector: a direction added to model activations to shift outputs in a desired way
steering vector: an intervention direction in representation space that nudges a neural model
representation manifold: a lower-dimensional geometric structure learned inside a high-dimensional model space
representation manifold: a curved structured region where a model organizes related internal states
representation manifold: a geometric surface or subspace that captures regularities in learned representations
weak constraint: a rule that preserves many compatible possibilities while still satisfying evidence
weak constraint: a restriction that rules out some cases while leaving broad freedom elsewhere
weak constraint: an underdetermining condition that fits observations without over-specifying the answer
simplicity bias: a preference for shorter or simpler forms when choosing explanations
simplicity bias: the tendency to favor compact explanations over more complex alternatives
simplicity bias: a selection pressure toward minimal descriptions or low-complexity hypotheses
validity gate: an explicit verifier that rejects explanations that fit observations for the wrong reason
validity gate: a test that filters candidate explanations by checking whether their fit is legitimate
validity gate: a criterion that blocks hypotheses whose apparent success comes from invalid shortcuts
regime transition: a change in the types, operations, or verifiers used to represent a scientific problem
regime transition: a shift to a new representational setup for pursuing discovery
regime transition: a move from one scientific search grammar to another with different artifacts or tests
schema revision: changing the representational vocabulary or artifact types used by a discovery system
schema revision: altering the concepts and structures used to encode a research problem
schema revision: updating the framework that decides what kinds of objects and relations can be represented
residual content: evidence or structure not explained by transporting the old representation into a new regime
residual content: the part of a result that remains unexplained by the previous schema
residual content: leftover structure that signals the old representation is missing something
self boundary: the modeled distinction between an agent and its environment
self boundary: the line a system draws between what counts as itself and what counts as outside
self boundary: an operational separation between an organism, agent, or model and its surroundings
autopoiesis: self-producing organization that maintains its own boundary and persistence
autopoiesis: a system process that regenerates the components and boundary that keep it alive
autopoiesis: self-maintaining organization in which a system continually produces its own conditions of existence
homeostasis: active regulation that keeps a system within viable bounds
homeostasis: the maintenance of internal stability through corrective regulation
homeostasis: feedback-driven control that preserves a system's viable operating range
valence: positive or negative value signal that guides action, attention, or adaptation
valence: the attractive or aversive value assigned to a state, object, or outcome
valence: a signed value signal that shapes preference, motivation, and behavior
